1: "In the year that King Uzziah died I saw the Lord sitting upon a throne, high and lifted up." John 12:41 — "Isaiah said these things because he saw his glory and spoke of him" — is the NT's explicit identification: the enthroned Lord of Isaiah 6:1 is Christ. John's context makes this unmistakable: he cites 6:10 in 12:40 and immediately identifies it as speaking of Christ's glory. The "high and lifted up" (rām wĕniśśāʾ) anticipates 52:13 where the Servant "shall be high and lifted up" using the same words — the throne vision and the Servant exaltation are the same divine glory seen from two vantage points.
2: "Above him stood the seraphim. Each had six wings: with two he covered his face, and with two he covered his feet, and with two he flew." The seraphim of Isaiah 6:2 become the four living creatures of Revelation 4:8 ("each of them with six wings, full of eyes all around and within") who encircle the throne of God and the Lamb. The seraphim's covering of faces before divine glory anticipates the NT theme of beholding glory directly: "we all, with unveiled face, beholding the glory of the Lord, are being transformed into the same image" (2 Corinthians 3:18).
3: "Holy, holy, holy is YHWH of hosts; the whole earth is full of his glory!" The trisagion is taken up in Revelation 4:8: "Holy, holy, holy, is the Lord God Almighty, who was and is and is coming!" — the Revelation version adds temporal extension that applies to Christ. "The whole earth full of his glory" is fulfilled in the incarnation: "the Word became flesh and dwelt among us, and we have seen his glory" (John 1:14). The seraphic proclamation is the worship of the pre-incarnate Son; John saw it and testified.
4: "And the foundations of the thresholds shook at the voice of him who called, and the house was filled with smoke." The threshold-shaking at the divine voice anticipates: the earthquake at the resurrection (Matthew 28:2); the Pentecost event where "suddenly there came from heaven a sound like a mighty rushing wind, and it filled the entire house" (Acts 2:2); and Revelation 15:8 where "the sanctuary was filled with smoke from the glory of God" — a deliberate echo of Isaiah 6:4 applied to the return of Christ's glory.
5: "Woe is me! For I am lost; for I am a man of unclean lips... for my eyes have seen the King, YHWH of hosts!" The creature's dissolution before the holy presence is consistently present in NT encounters with Christ: Peter's "Depart from me, for I am a sinful man, O Lord" (Luke 5:8); John falling "as though dead" before the risen Christ (Revelation 1:17); Paul blinded on the Damascus road (Acts 9:3-4). The "unclean lips" healed by the coal (Isaiah 6:7) anticipate Christ's purification of the unclean at his touch (Mark 1:40-42).
6: "Then one of the seraphim flew to me, having in his hand a burning coal that he had taken with tongs from the altar." The seraph mediates purification from the altar — the altar being the place of sacrifice. Hebrews 9:14 ("the blood of Christ... purify our conscience from dead works") continues this altar-to-purification logic. The flight of the seraph with the coal connects to the proclamation of the gospel by those sent (Romans 10:15, "how beautiful are the feet of those who preach the good news").
7: "He touched my mouth and said: 'Behold, this has touched your lips; your guilt is taken away, and your sin atoned for.'" The touch that removes guilt and atones is the purification that precedes commission. In the NT, forgiveness precedes and grounds mission: "your sins are forgiven... go in peace" (Luke 7:48-50). Jesus touches the untouchable (lepers, the dead) and pronounces forgiveness and healing — the coal-touch of Isaiah 6:7 becomes the healing touch of Christ. The atonement vocabulary (kuppār) connects to the priestly Christology of Hebrews 9:26.
8: "And I heard the voice of the Lord saying, 'Whom shall I send, and who will go for us?' Then I said, 'Here I am! Send me.'" The divine council's "us" and the prophet's commission anticipate the Trinitarian sending. John 17:18 — "as you sent me into the world, so I have sent them into the world" — continues the pattern: the Father sends the Son (John 20:21), the Son sends the Spirit (John 15:26). Christ's "Behold, I have come to do your will, O God" (Hebrews 10:7, citing Psalms 40:7-8) is the full embodiment of the prophetic "Here I am."
9: "Go, and say to this people: 'Keep on hearing, but do not understand; keep on seeing, but do not perceive.'" This is the most frequently cited OT text in the NT: Matthew 13:14-15; Mark 4:12; Luke 8:10; John 12:40; Acts 28:26-27; Romans 11:8. The programmatic hardening commission begins with the Servant's own ministry — the parables function to hide meaning from those who refuse to see. The hardening is judicial: persistent rejection of light results in inability to perceive it. John 12:40 adds: "Isaiah said these things because he saw his glory and spoke of him" — the one who hardens is Christ himself, as he was the Lord in Isaiah 6:1.
10: "Make the heart of this people dull, and their ears heavy, and blind their eyes; lest they see with their eyes, and hear with their ears, and understand with their hearts, and turn and be healed." John 12:40 quotes this verse and adds: "Isaiah said these things because he saw his glory and spoke of him" (Isaiah 12:41) — making the hardening the judgment of the pre-incarnate Christ. The healing that hardening prevents is the healing Jesus offers throughout his ministry. Matthew 13:15 ends the citation with the positive: "lest they turn, and I would heal them" — the healing remains possible for those who turn; the hardening terminal only for final refusal.
11: "Then I said, 'How long, O Lord?' And he said: 'Until cities lie waste without inhabitant.'" The "how long?" question is the eschatological lament. In Revelation 6:10, the souls under the altar cry "how long, O Lord, holy and true, before you will judge and avenge our blood?" — the prophetic "how long?" of Isaiah 6:11 becomes the martyrs' cry. Christ's own "how long am I to bear with you?" (Matthew 17:17) echoes the divine patience. The answer in both Isaiah and Revelation: until the full measure of judgment is accomplished.
12: "And YHWH removes people far away, and the forsaken places are many in the midst of the land." The scattering judgment is the reverse of the promised regathering. In NT terms: "I have other sheep that are not of this fold. I must bring them also" (John 10:16). The "forsaken places" become the sites of the new community — Acts 8:4, "now those who were scattered went about preaching the word," turns the dispersion into mission, fulfilling the gathering of the scattered that Isaiah promised.
13: "And though a tenth remain in it, it will be burned again, like a terebinth or an oak, whose stump remains when it is felled. The holy seed is its stump." The holy seed (zeraʿ qōdeš) is the remnant Paul addresses in Romans 11:5: "at the present time there is a remnant, chosen by grace." The stump also anticipates the Servant: 11:1 describes "a shoot from the stump of Jesse" — the Messiah from the apparently dead royal line. The cutting-down and sprouting is the death-and-resurrection pattern: the holy seed that falls into the ground and dies bears much fruit (John 12:24).
